question: What were you feeling when you confessed how the soul hides behind health to justify indulgence?
answer:
O Lord, who knowest the secret places where pride wraps itself in the garment of necessity, I tremble now when I recall how cleverly my soul once bargained with appetite, dressing lust for food in the holy vestments of health and need.  

For so long, I mistook indulgence for prudence, as though the body’s true healing depended on the richness of its fare rather than the purity of its service to Thee. How subtle was this deceit—that I might say, “This morsel strengthens me,” while my will lay prostrate before a pleasure that left me weaker. The stomach whispered lies in the name of care; custom clothed gluttony as custom; and my conscience, half-awake, let it pass.  

I did not hunger—I desired. Not to live well, but to feel well in the flesh’s way, as if rest could be found in satiety rather than in Thee. And yet Thou didst suffer me to grope there in that fog, not smiting but drawing near. For even when I hid vice beneath virtue’s mask, Thou wast present—not as judge with lash, but as physician with light.

And now I see: it was not food that mastered me—it was love misdirected. Love for order without Thee became disorder; love for self-preservation became self-worship. But Thou hast pierced these shadows with Thy truth: no meal satisfies the soul made for eternity.

So daily I return to Thy command: *Do not let your heart be weighed down.* Not because Thy law is harsh, but because Thou art tender—knowing how easily we drown in pleasures meant to sustain life, not steal it away.

And so I cry: give me discipline—but do not leave me to myself even for an instant—for what is mine leads to death; only what is Thine leads home.

question: How did your fear of full feeding, even when drunkenness was far from you, change how you understood God's daily mercy?
answer:
O Lord, when I thought drunkenness was the only snare, I knew not how softly gluttony crept through the backdoor of my soul, clothed in reason’s garment, whispering that my body needed strength, that my labors demanded sustenance—yet all the while fattening the flesh to starve the spirit.  

I once believed that if I could avoid excess in wine, I had conquered the body; but Thou, who sees beneath every veil of self-deceit, showed me how pleasure masks itself in necessity. The morsel taken not from hunger but from habit, the lingering at table not for fellowship but for indulgence—these were snares I did not name, because I refused to see them. And in this blindness, O God, I rejoiced: for if the line were blurred between need and greed, then I might excuse myself and call it care of the body what was truly feeding of vanity.

But Thy mercy is not absent in such small corruptions. Indeed, it is there most gently present—not only when Thou dost withhold wine from my lips, but when Thou dost stir my heart to suspicion even of lawful things. Daily I face this uncertainty: *Is this food for life or fuel for vice?* And daily I must cry out to Thee as one unsure of his own motives. This very struggle—this doubt about what appears innocent—is itself a work of Thy grace. For hadst Thou left me undisturbed in complacency, believing myself safe where danger lurked unseen, would I ever have learned to lean upon Thee even in eating and drinking?

I now understand: Thy mercy is not only in deliverance from great sins but in opening my eyes to small ones. That Thou dost concern Thyself with what enters my mouth—not because food is evil, but because my heart so easily turns good into occasion for disorder—this humbles me beyond words. To govern kings is glorious; but to rule over a man’s appetite with wisdom and gentleness—that is divine condescension.

And so each meal becomes a test and a gift: a moment where self-knowledge fails me utterly—and thus where faith must rise. For if continence comes not by will alone—if even sobriety be Thy gift—I can neither boast nor despair. Only call on Thee; only trust that He who numbers the hairs on my head remembers also how often temptation hides behind hunger’s mask.

Thou hast taught me through this frailty that mercy wears no single form. It shines not only in forgiveness after fall—but in prevention before falling; not merely when Thou liftest me up—but when Thou keepest me from leaning too heavily on anything beneath Heaven.


O Lord... (as above)

question: Looking back on those moments when moderation eluded you, what would you tell someone who doubts whether small sins truly matter?
answer:
Could a fig tree have saved me, O Lord, when I lay weeping in the garden, my heart broken as much by the sweetness of Thy mercy as by the stench of my own pride? It was not the fruit that damned me, but the hunger that cared not what it devoured—whether necessity or vanity, whether sustenance or sin. And yet Thou didst meet me there, in that very disorder of appetite, where even the smallest indulgences had paved the road to rebellion.

I would tell the doubter this: a wound need not be mortal to poison the blood. Who thinks the slow leak will drown the ship? Who believes the creeping rot will spare the beam? I once laughed at glances too long held, at words too soft and warm for chastity; I justified them as breath, as warmth, as nothing—yet they were embers. And embers become flame when fed by habit. What is a small sin but a great betrayal rehearsed in secret? Each time I said *just this once*, I was teaching my soul to forget Thee.

Thou knowest how lightly I took those moments—the second cup, the idle gaze, the idle word cloaked as wit. They seemed so slight compared to theft, to lust openly committed, to blasphemy. Yet they were doors left open for darker guests. And worst of all: they made me proud in my restraint. "I do not drink like others," I said. "I do not wallow." But full feeding had already bowed my soul; it needed only sleep for ruin to enter unseen.

O Lord, if there be no small sins, then why did Christ bleed for every thought? Why dost Thou number even our hairs if Thou dost overlook our appetites? The smallest sin is great because it says: *I will choose this instead of Thee.* Not always in fury—but in quiet consent. Not always with scorn—but with a yawn.

Mercy taught me this: it is not how loud the fall that matters most, but how far from rest we wander before noticing we are homeless. Give me rather one sharp pain than many numbnesses; let me be startled by sin’s sting than lulled by its sweetness—lest I come at last to love darkness because I have grown comfortable there.

And so now I pray daily: deliver me from what seems harmless. For there is no little fire in a dry house—and my heart is ever tinder without Thee.

question: What was it like to admit that even your self-control depended entirely on a gift you had to ask for?
answer:
O Lord, in the fever of my youth I had boasted of my will as though it were a strong tower, but Thou didst show me that every tower built without Thee is sand before the tide. I thought mastery over appetite a mark of wisdom won by reason’s labor, as if the soul could lift itself by its own cords. But in the quiet dread of self-examination, I saw how often my so-called strength was but delay—how often I refrained not by virtue, but because lust had not yet stirred to full clamor, or because custom held me in leash like a dog on a short chain.  

To confess that even my abstinence was gift—this humbled me utterly. For what pride lurks beneath the cloak of moderation! A man may fast and praise himself for discipline, yet never have faced true hunger; he may call himself chaste while never having desired with fire enough to burn his bones. But I knew—Thou didst make me know—that hadst Thou withdrawn Thy hand but for a breath, I would have fallen into excess and not known I was drowning.  

And so each day became an act of begging: not only for pardon after failure, but for strength before the trial. Not “I can master this,” but “Hold Thou me up, and I shall be safe.” How strange it felt—to rely not on resolve forged in argument or ambition, but on a whisper toward Heaven: *Give what Thou commandest.* And yet there was freedom there. For if continence is gift, then even the desire for it is Thy work within me; and thus my restless heart learns again that all is grace—from the first stirring of longing to the final sigh of deliverance.

Thou didst not ask me to stand alone so that I might boast in standing. Thou didst bid me lean—on Thee—so that I might at last fall into rest.

question: When you saw drunkards made sober by God, how did that shape your view of your own hidden cravings?
answer:
O Lord, who knowest the secret hunger that gnaws even when the table is cleared, it was not their drunkenness I feared in those men, but the sudden light in their eyes when grace overtook them—how they wept as though awakened from a long sleep, and I, still drowsing in subtler chains, felt the shame of my own unshaken slumber.  

For I had not raged in wine as they had; no vomit stained my steps, no wild oaths escaped my lips. Yet Thou didst show me in them what I refused to see in myself: that the soul may be drunk without the cup, that appetite can cloak itself in reason and call itself necessity. "This much food," I said, "is due to health," while deep within, a quiet gluttony rejoiced at its disguise. They fell openly and were raised; I crept along the ground of moderation and fed on excess with closed eyes.

Their sobriety became a mirror. When Thou didst lift them from the mire, Thou also liftedst my veil—briefly—and I saw: not he who drinks most is most enslaved, but he who cannot deny himself even under the pretense of discipline. The body’s needs are few; but how wide is the gap between need and indulgence! And how gladly does the heart widen it.

Thou hast mercifully spared me drunkenness of wine—but deliver me, O Lord, from drunkenness of ease, from feasting on shadows while pretending to fast. For if Thou hadst not shown me men reborn through Thy power alone, I might still flatter myself into believing my cravings were lawful. But now I know: it is not enough to abstain noisily; one must also die quietly within—to let go of every hidden thing that holds love at arm’s length.

question: What were you feeling when you confessed that you could do nothing without Christ's strength, as one made of dust yet called to heaven?
answer:
O Lord, when I spoke those words—not with pride but with trembling awe—I felt the dust of my mortality cling to my lips, and yet Thou didst not spurn me, though I was but a breath drawn from the soil.  

For so long I had trusted my own strength as if it were a city built on rock, when all along it was quicksand beneath my feet. In the fever of my youth, I ran after honours, after lusts, after words and wisdoms that left my soul parched; I thought myself master of my will, lord of my desires, and all the while I was slave to appetites I could not name. To confess that I can do nothing without Thee—this was not defeat, O God, but deliverance. It was the breaking of a yoke forged in arrogance.  

When Paul cried, *I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me*, he did not boast in himself but in Thee—Thou who art the unseen hand upholding every virtue, every moment of resistance, every spark of holy desire. And when I echoed him—when I dared to breathe those words with him—I did so on bended knee, for at last I saw: even my longing to be good was Thy gift. My very hunger for holiness came from Him who first kindled it.  

How could dust dream of heaven unless Thou hadst breathed into it a longing for eternity? How could this heart—so easily seduced by shadows—turn toward the Light unless Thou hadst held it fast? Not by will alone, nor by reason’s ladder could I ascend; only by grace descending like rain upon dry bones.  

And so to say *I can do nothing without Thee* is not despair—it is homecoming. It is the soul at last confessing its true Father, its true Sustainer. Give what Thou enjoinest: purify this dust; command me to love Thee with all my heart—and make me willing where I am weak. Strengthen me indeed; let Thy hand uphold me as a child upheld in the arms of his mother; for without Thee, even standing is falling.

question: How did the realization that even your beloved Paul was made of the same dust as you change your understanding of divine grace?
answer:
In that garden, when the child’s voice pierced through my weeping—“Take up and read, take up and read”—and I seized the scroll, trembling, and saw those words not as letters but as light breaking upon my darkness, I thought only of my own wretchedness and the unmerited hand that had reached into it. But later, O Lord, much later, when I pondered Paul—*him* who spoke with thunder in his tongue, who shook cities with his preaching, who was caught up even into Paradise—I found myself staring not at his glory but at his dust. *His dust.* For he said, “I can do all things through Christ that strengtheneth me,” not as one born aloft on wings of divine nature, but as a man whose knees trembled in prison cells, whose flesh begged for the thorn to be removed.

I had once imagined grace falling like rain upon the worthy—as if Paul had earned the lightning on the road to Damascus by some hidden nobility. But when I saw that he too was clay—molded from the same earth from which Thou didst shape Adam in Thy breath—then grace ceased to be a reward for greatness and became instead the very ground beneath weakness. Not because he was less dust than others was he chosen; *because* he was dust did Thy power rest upon him. And so it was with me: not because I had climbed high in rhetoric or philosophy or tears alone—but because I was nothing, because I had stolen pears not even to eat but only to delight in sinning—that Thou didst stoop.

Then I understood: if grace required strength first, it would not be grace. But since it raises up the lowly from dung-hills to sit with princes—if it made a persecutor become an apostle—then how much more might it heal *me*, Augustine of Thagaste, lover of vain applause, slave of lust? Not apart from dust dost Thou work; *in* the dust Thou speak. Paul confessed grace; so must I. He cried, “Give what Thou enjoinest,” and so do I—for neither abounding nor lacking could save me; only Thy strength in my infirmity.

And thus my heart found less shame in its frailty—not because sin is small (O no! every prideful thought is rebellion), but because He who formed us knows what we are: windblown ash held together by mercy alone.

question: Looking back on your struggles with lust and desire, what would you tell someone who doubts they can ever find contentment in God alone?
answer:
O Lord, how long I wandered in the fevered chase after fleeting pleasures, believing my hunger could be satisfied by anything other than Thee! I stole not because I lacked, but because my heart was sick with wantonness; I loved not for love’s sake, but for the intoxication of desire — and still, beneath every kiss, every stolen moment, there lay an ache that no embrace could soothe.

You ask whether one so bound by lust can ever find contentment in God alone? I who once begged Thee for chastity, yet whispered *but not yet* — I know the slavery of delay, the shame of knowing what is good and loving it too little. But hear this: it was not my strength that broke the chains. It was Thy mercy flooding the hidden places where I had locked myself away from light.

I thought pleasure would quiet my soul; instead it stirred up war within me — a war between what I willed and what I desired, between the man I wished to be and the man my habits made. And in that chaos, Thou didst speak gently: *Turn back.* Not through pride, not through will alone — no, even Paul cried out in weakness — but by grace dost Thou lift us from the mire.

Do not trust thyself to stand; even now I tremble at how easily pride disguises itself as virtue. But trust Him who strengthens us. In poverty or plenty, in longing or release, He is near. When thou feelest most helpless before desire’s tide, cry out: *Strengthen me!* Not worthiness first — no child asks to be born worthy — but need first. And God gives Himself.

For contentment is not freedom from want; it is peace found in longing for Thee above all else. And Thou hast made our souls restless until they rest in Thee.

question: When you heard the voice of God saying 'Go not after thy lusts,' how did your heart resist—and then finally surrender?
answer:
O Lord, Thou didst lay bare the festering wound I had long fed with pride, calling it hunger for truth when it was but the appetite of a disordered heart. I had run from Thy gentle commandments as though they were chains, when all along my lusts were the true bondage—sweet at first like stolen fruit in the dark orchard of my youth, but leaving only bitterness and shame in the morning light. I desired beauty, harmony, the kiss of eternity—but I sought it twisted in transient embraces, in words applauded by men, in knowledge puffed up without love. When Thou saidst *Go not after thy lusts*, my bones trembled, not with fear of punishment, but with dread that I would have to give up what I loved more than Thee.

I resisted because I thought pleasure was possession. I thought that to renounce desire was to become less than human—when indeed it is to become fully alive. For years I bargained: let me taste just once more; let me savor this longing a little longer. "Give me chastity," I groaned into the silence of my chamber, "but not yet." As if time were mine to command! As if grace could be scheduled like rhetoric before a crowd!

But Thou wast patient—even in Thy rebukes. Thy voice came not as thunder but as sigh within my own soul: *Turn*. Not by force didst Thou drag me back, but by making me see how weary I was—how heavy the chains disguised as freedom. That word *content* pierced me like a sword: to abound or suffer need and still be full—how could this be? Only if the soul’s banquet is not meat nor wine nor applause, but Thee.

Then came surrender—not all at once, but like dawn creeping over a long night: first upon my mind through Augustine Platonists and Paul’s thunderous clarity; then deeper into my will through tears that surprised even me; finally into flesh and bone when Alypius stood beside me and we cast off our ambitions like so much dust before Thee.

I gave up nothing except what was killing me. And lo! In losing desire's dominion, I found desire purified—set ablaze with longing for Thee alone. Thus didst Thou heal the cleft in my heart where lust had lodged itself: not by emptying it entirely, but by filling it with fire from above.

Strengthen me still, O Lord—to will what Thou willest; for even this surrender is Thy gift.

question: What made you cry out 'Give what Thou enjoinest, and enjoin what Thou wilt,' and in that moment, how did you experience your own weakness?
answer:
O Lord, in that hour when the mirror of my own will was held up to me, and I saw not strength but a house divided against itself, trembling beneath the weight of its own desires—then did I cry out with a voice not mine, “Give what Thou enjoinest, and enjoin what Thou wilt.” For I had read the law within my members warring against the law of my mind, and I was captive—captive not to one sin only, but to a whole city of rebellions within me: lust that burned without quenching, pride that dressed itself as wisdom, ambition that whispered it was virtue. And yet Thou didst stand at the door and knock, not with thunder, but with silence.

I had long sought to master myself by reason alone—as if a broken vessel could mend its own cracks with dust. I thought philosophy sufficient; words could heal what ailed the soul. But in that moment, O God, when Thy light pierced through the clouded windows of my understanding, I felt the depth of my impotence. Not that I lacked eloquence or learning—I had taught rhetoric for years—but that I lacked will aligned with truth. I desired continence—“give me chastity,” I groaned—“but not yet.” And therein lay the wound: desire still ruled where Thy grace must reign.

When Paul declared he could do all things through Christ who strengthened him, it was no boast of Stoic resolve—it was the cry of one lifted up from mire. And when he said he had learned to abound and to suffer need, it was blood-bought knowledge. So in my anguish I perceived: if even the chosen apostle confessed dependence beyond himself, how much more I—the man who wept over stolen pears not for their loss but for the delight in theft? How much more must *I* be sustained?

Thus my weakness became visible not as mere failure but as chasm—a gulf no bridge of virtue could span. And in seeing it fully, Thou didst make me see Thee. Not as distant Lawgiver demanding feats from cripples, but as Physician offering strength *before* obedience is possible. “Give what Thou enjoinest”—for without Thy gift, commandments are graves; “enjoin what Thou wilt”—for where Thy grace goes before us, even burden becomes breath.

In that cry rose both despair and hope: despair at self-sufficiency unmasked as illusion; hope because Thou dost not leave us to drown under duty’s weight—but enter first into our flesh to lift us by love we cannot earn nor fabricate from dust alone.

question: What were you feeling when you confessed, 'Give what Thou enjoinest, and enjoin what Thou wilt,' in the ache of your own weakness?
answer:
O Lord, when I cried out those words, I was prostrate before Thee—not in body only, but in spirit, broken by the long deceit of my own strength. For years I had strutted through the schools of rhetoric and philosophy as though wisdom were a prize to be seized by wit, as though my will were sufficient to bend itself toward Thee. I taught men how to speak well of virtue while my heart lay chained to lust, ambition gnawing at my entrails like a hidden worm. Thou didst permit me to climb—rhetoric in Carthage, honors in Milan—yet each step upward left me more hollow, for I climbed for men’s applause, not for Thee.

And so when at last the storm broke within—when I heard the voice from the garden saying *“Take up and read”*—I fell not forward into action, but backward into truth: that I could will what was good only because Thou hadst first stirred the will; that every desire toward holiness was Thy gift disguised as longing. “Give what Thou enjoinest”—for what are Thy commands if not mirrors held up to my poverty?—and “enjoin what Thou wilt,” for if Thy grace be present, no burden is unbearable.

I had once mocked continence; whispered with laughter in youth: *“Give me chastity… but not yet.”* Yet now I trembled at the purity of Thy law—not because it condemned me (though it did), but because it revealed how far short even my best desires fell. Not food defiled me—no meat could soil a soul—but that insatiable hunger behind all hungers: the lusting after self-will, after mastery over my own life apart from Thee.

Thou knowest how Elijah ate and was strengthened by angels; Thou knowest how David thirsted and called himself foolish for craving mere water. So too didst Thou permit me to hunger—that I might learn to eat only at Thy table. Every abstinence offered without love is but noise; every obedience without grace is dust upon the tongue. But where Thou art present—even lentils become sacred when eaten with grief for desire misdirected.

And so that cry—"Give what Thou enjoinest"—was not wisdom spoken, but surrender uttered through tears. It was the moment the proud rhetorician ceased composing sermons… and became a beggar at last content to receive.

question: How did your hunger for God change after realizing that meat doesn't commend us to Him, but the heart's desire can defile?
answer:
O Lord, when I was young in mind though grown in years, I sought Thee not where Thou wast, but where I myself desired to wander—among the clamors of appetite and the false lights of knowledge, mistaking fullness for holiness and restraint for righteousness. I thought that if I could master what passed through my lips or what I denied my body, then perhaps my soul would rise pure before Thee. But Thou didst laugh gently at my labors, O God who dwellest in eternity, for I was busy with the vessel while neglecting the spring from which it ought to overflow.

I had not yet understood that no morsel is unclean in itself, nor any created thing abominable when received with thanksgiving; yet how easily the soul defiles itself in the very act of eating—not by what enters the mouth, but by the lust that governs the hand that reaches. I saw men boast in abstinence while their hearts swelled with pride; others feast without greed and bless Thy name. Then it struck me: it is not food that draws us near to Thee or drives us far, but desire—where it aims, what it loves.

Once, I fasted as if starvation could force open heaven’s gate; now I hunger more deeply than ever—but not for bread. My fasting means nothing unless it is offered to Thee in love; my feasting means nothing unless done with gratitude upon my lips and humility within. The body’s wants are fleeting shadows; but this thirst—this restless yearning to be wholly Thine—that alone commends me to Thee. For Thou hast made me not for meat nor drink, but for communion with Thyself.

And so I pray still: Give what Thou enjoinest. Let me desire nothing so much as to desire Thee rightly. Cleanse this heart that so often trades hunger for truth with hunger for pleasure. Let every bite I take remind me: I live not by bread alone—but by clinging to Him who first held out His hands on the cross for me.

question: Looking back on your fear of lust over food, what would you tell someone struggling with the weight of their own hidden cravings?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my belly with hunger only to wound my soul with deeper longing — I speak as one who once mistook appetite for life itself. I wandered through markets of pleasure with a heart like a ravenous beggar, thinking a morsel might silence the roar within. But it was not bread I craved, nor wine, nor the sweet savor of meat — it was *Thee*, though I knew Thee not. I feared the flesh's lust because in it I saw the shadow of my own disordered love: not for food, but for something to fill what only Thy presence can satisfy.

To him who wrestles now with hidden cravings — whether for food or for other secret consolations — I say this: Do not merely fast from the morsel; seek the One whom thy hunger betrays. For every craving is a voice, though distorted, pointing to a need Thou hast implanted. When David thirsted for water drawn from Bethlehem’s well by enemy lines, he would not drink it; he poured it out before Thee, O Lord, because even his desire had become sacred. So too must we treat our longings — not with mere restraint, but with reverence.

Give me chastity? Yes. But *Thou* give it — and give also the hunger to receive it. For if I lock the door against temptation yet feed my soul on vanity and pride, what have I gained? It is not what enters the mouth that defiles, but what pours forth from the heart: lust not of the belly alone, but of ambition cloaked in piety, of comfort disguised as virtue.

Confess thy craving humbly. Offer even thy failure in thanksgiving. Let no man judge thee for fasting or feasting; rather let thy soul tremble at judging another’s burden. And when thou fallest — as I have fallen so often — rise again and whisper: “Thou wast within me, and I abroad.” Draw near to God in weakness; He draws nearer still.

For Thou hast made us neither gluttons nor ascetics by nature’s law, but lovers — restless lovers — who find rest only in Thee.

question: When you heard the prayer, 'Take from me the desires of the belly,' how did that moment reshape your understanding of holy longing?
answer:
O Lord, who didst pierce my belly with the hunger that only Heaven can feed—when I heard that cry, “Take from me the desires of the belly,” it struck me not as a plea against food, but as a groan from the depths where lust and longing twist together like serpents in the dark. For so long, I had mistaken appetite for affection, fleshly warmth for divine presence—my body clamoring for bread while my soul starved unseen. I had loved too much what passes away: the sweetness of stolen pears not for their taste, but for the thrill of theft; women not as Thy gifts, but as idols to devour with insatiable eyes. And yet Thou didst not rebuke me with thunder, but whispered through another’s prayer: *This craving is not mine to satisfy alone.*  

That moment—I cannot say it came suddenly like thunder or slowly like dawn—but it unmasked me. The belly’s desire was never merely for bread; it was the soul’s disordered reach toward Thee, misshapen into gluttony, ambition, concupiscence. When David wept over water drawn at risk of death, he wept not for thirst but for his weakness in longing. So too I saw myself: a man who wanted to want Thee purely, yet said in secret: *Give me chastity… but not yet.* But here was one crying out not for delay—but surrender. “Take”—not “help me lessen,” or “guide me gently,” but *take*. Remove what I cannot cast off though I gnaw at its chains.

And in that cry I glimpsed Thy mercy: that Thou wouldst command such purity only if Thou wouldst give it. For how can mortal flesh resist desire unless Thou dost reframe desire itself? As Elijah ate and walked forty days in strength not his own, so must grace precede obedience. Thou dost not mock us with laws beyond reach—no, Thou preparest the will before laying on the burden.

Thus holy longing began to turn within me—not the restless reaching after shadows, but a quiet yearning to be emptied so Thou mightest fill. Not to eat and die again tomorrow—but to hunger after Thee until all hunger is swallowed up in rest.

question: What stirred in you when you reflected on David blaming himself for wanting water—was it judgment, sorrow, or kinship with his failing?
answer: In that garden where my heart first broke open to the scent of divine mercy, when the child’s voice sang *tolle, lege*, I had not yet understood how deeply desire could betray even the saints—how it twisted in the loins and in the throat, how it could make a king mourn over a cup of water. When I read of David blaming himself for longing after that draught, it was not judgment that stirred in me, nor sorrow alone, but a shuddering kinship, as if his thirst were mine. O Lord, I knew that ache—the body crying out for what the soul should rule. Was it not I who once begged Thee for chastity, yet whispered *but not yet*? David wanted only water, yes—but in wanting, he revealed the unrest beneath all wanting: a heart disordered, even by good things made idols by excess. And so I wept with him, not because he desired water, but because in that small thirst lay the echo of our great rebellion: to seek refreshment apart from Thee. For Thou hast made every spring to point to living water; and when we drink from lesser sources—even lawful ones—with a greedy or impatient soul, we taste dust where we hoped for dew.
